questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: Which of the following best describes the purpose of a program?

choices.text: The reason the program was created and the problem it is meant to solve
choices.explanation: Program purpose is WHY the program exists — the problem it is meant to solve or the goal it aims to achieve.

choices.text: The specific steps a program follows when it executes
choices.explanation: This describes the program's algorithm or function — the 'what' — not the purpose, which is the 'why' behind its creation.

choices.text: The programming language used to write the program
choices.explanation: The programming language is a tool used to write the program, not its purpose.

choices.text: The number of lines of code in the program
choices.explanation: The number of lines of code is a characteristic of the implementation, not related to the program's purpose.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: What is the difference between program function and program purpose?

choices.text: Program function is the cost of developing the program; program purpose is the intended audience
choices.explanation: Neither definition is correct — cost and audience are not what these terms mean in computer science.

choices.text: Program function is what the program does when it runs; program purpose is why the program was created
choices.explanation: Function describes the observable behavior (what it does); purpose describes the reason it was built (why it was created).

choices.text: Program function is why the program was created; program purpose is what the program does when it runs
choices.explanation: These definitions are swapped — function = what it does, purpose = why it exists.

choices.text: Program function and program purpose are the same thing
choices.explanation: These are distinct concepts — a program's purpose (reason it was created) can differ significantly from its function (actual behavior).

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a nonphysical computing innovation?

choices.text: A self-driving car
choices.explanation: A self-driving car is a physical computing innovation — it is a tangible vehicle with embedded computing systems.

choices.text: A smartphone
choices.explanation: A smartphone is a physical computing innovation — it has a tangible, hardware form.

choices.text: A social media platform
choices.explanation: A social media platform exists purely as software and digital services with no physical form, making it a nonphysical computing innovation.

choices.text: A fitness tracking wristband
choices.explanation: A fitness wristband is a physical computing innovation — it has a tangible hardware component worn on the wrist.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: A student is developing a game app. She builds the main menu first, tests it thoroughly, then adds the game logic, tests it, and continues one feature at a time. Which development approach is she using?

choices.text: Iterative development
choices.explanation: Iterative development involves repeatedly refining a full or partial program based on feedback — not building strictly one feature at a time.

choices.text: Code review
choices.explanation: Code review is when another person examines code for errors — it is not a development approach for sequencing features.

choices.text: Pair programming
choices.explanation: Pair programming is a collaboration technique involving two programmers at one workstation — unrelated to how features are sequenced.

choices.text: Incremental development
choices.explanation: Incremental development adds and tests one feature at a time before building the next, exactly as described.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: Which of the following best describes iterative development?

choices.text: Repeatedly making small improvements and refining a program based on testing and feedback
choices.explanation: Iterative development cycles through design, implementation, and testing repeatedly, refining the program with each pass.

choices.text: Adding one feature at a time and testing each feature before moving on
choices.explanation: This describes incremental development, not iterative development.

choices.text: Building a complete program all at once, then testing it at the end
choices.explanation: This describes a waterfall approach, not iterative development — iterative development involves repeated cycles of improvement.

choices.text: Having two programmers write the same program simultaneously and combining the best parts
choices.explanation: This does not describe any standard development methodology — pair programming has one person writing at a time.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: Which of the following is an example of pair programming?

choices.text: Two programmers each write their own versions of a program, then compare results
choices.explanation: This describes parallel independent development, not pair programming.

choices.text: One programmer writes code while another programmer reviews it at the same workstation in real time
choices.explanation: Pair programming places two developers at one workstation simultaneously — one types (driver) while the other reviews (navigator).

choices.text: Two programmers review each other's independently written code after it is completed
choices.explanation: This describes a code review process, not pair programming, which requires working together in real time.

choices.text: A programmer uses two monitors to write code faster
choices.explanation: Using multiple monitors is a hardware setup choice and has nothing to do with pair programming.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: Which TWO of the following are benefits of collaboration during program development? Select two answers.

choices.text: Identifying and fixing errors that an individual programmer might miss
choices.explanation: Collaboration allows different perspectives to catch bugs and logic errors that one developer overlooking their own assumptions would miss.

choices.text: Guaranteeing that the program will have no errors after release
choices.explanation: Collaboration reduces errors but cannot guarantee zero errors — no development process eliminates all bugs.

choices.text: Bringing diverse perspectives that can lead to more creative and inclusive solutions
choices.explanation: Diverse collaborators bring different experiences and ideas, leading to better solutions that serve a wider range of users.

choices.text: Eliminating the need for testing and debugging
choices.explanation: Collaboration does not replace testing — programs still require systematic testing and debugging regardless of team size.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: A programmer writes a program that compiles and runs without any error messages, but always calculates the wrong answer for a given set of inputs. What type of error does the program contain?

choices.text: Syntax error
choices.explanation: Syntax errors prevent the program from compiling or running at all — the program would not execute with a syntax error.

choices.text: Runtime error
choices.explanation: Runtime errors cause the program to crash during execution — this program runs without crashing, so the error is not a runtime error.

choices.text: Logic error
choices.explanation: A logic error allows the program to run without crashing but produces incorrect results — the program's reasoning is flawed.

choices.text: Overflow error
choices.explanation: An overflow error occurs when a value exceeds the allocated memory space — it can cause crashes or incorrect values but is a specific type, not the general description given.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: A student writes a program but forgets a required closing brace. The program will not run at all. What type of error has occurred?

choices.text: Runtime error
choices.explanation: Runtime errors occur during execution — a missing brace prevents the program from running at all, so the error is detected before execution.

choices.text: Logic error
choices.explanation: Logic errors occur in running programs that produce wrong results — missing syntax prevents execution entirely.

choices.text: Overflow error
choices.explanation: Overflow errors involve numbers exceeding memory limits during calculations — not missing punctuation.

choices.text: Syntax error
choices.explanation: A missing closing brace violates the grammar rules of the language — this is a syntax error that prevents the program from running.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: A program crashes unexpectedly when a user tries to access the fifth item in a three-item list. What type of error is this?

choices.text: Runtime error
choices.explanation: Trying to access an index that does not exist causes the program to crash during execution — this is a runtime error.

choices.text: Logic error
choices.explanation: Logic errors produce incorrect output without crashing — this program crashed, which is characteristic of a runtime error.

choices.text: Syntax error
choices.explanation: Syntax errors prevent the program from running at all — the program ran and then crashed, ruling out a syntax error.

choices.text: Design error
choices.explanation: Design error is not a standard error classification in CS — the error is specifically a runtime error caused by invalid list access.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: Which of the following best describes the role of documentation in a program?

choices.text: It speeds up program execution
choices.explanation: Documentation has no effect on execution speed — it is text for human readers, not executable code.

choices.text: It explains what the program does and how to use it, for current and future programmers
choices.explanation: Documentation communicates program purpose, design decisions, and usage instructions to anyone reading or maintaining the code.

choices.text: It automatically finds and fixes errors in the program
choices.explanation: Documentation is written by developers to explain code — it does not debug or fix errors automatically.

choices.text: It replaces the need for testing
choices.explanation: Documentation and testing serve entirely different purposes — documentation does not verify that code works correctly.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: When a programmer uses code from an online repository in their project, what should they do according to best practices and ethical guidelines?

choices.text: Modify the code slightly so it is considered original work
choices.explanation: Minor modifications do not make borrowed code original — the original author still deserves credit through acknowledgment.

choices.text: Delete all comments from the borrowed code to hide its origin
choices.explanation: Removing attribution to hide borrowed code is unethical and may violate the original license terms.

choices.text: Acknowledge the source in their program documentation
choices.explanation: Proper acknowledgment credits the original creator, which is an ethical requirement and often a legal one under licensing terms.

choices.text: Re-write the code from memory after reading it
choices.explanation: Reproducing code from memory after reading it is still derived from the original — acknowledgment is still appropriate.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: Which of the following is an example of an event in a computing context?

choices.text: A variable storing the value 5
choices.explanation: Storing a value in a variable is a state, not an event — events are actions that trigger responses.

choices.text: The operating system allocating memory to a process
choices.explanation: Memory allocation is an internal system process, not an event in the programming sense of a user-triggered action.

choices.text: A program printing its output
choices.explanation: Printing output is a program action, not an event — an event is something that happens to trigger program behavior, not a result of it.

choices.text: A user clicking a button on a webpage
choices.explanation: A button click is a classic event — an action detected by the program that triggers a specific response or behavior.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: What is the purpose of version control in software development?

choices.text: To track changes to a codebase over time and allow programmers to revert to previous versions
choices.explanation: Version control systems record a history of all changes, enabling teams to view, compare, and restore previous states of the code.

choices.text: To automatically test code for bugs
choices.explanation: Version control tracks changes to code — automated testing is a separate tool/process.

choices.text: To ensure a program runs on all operating systems
choices.explanation: Cross-platform compatibility is addressed by the programming language and build tools — not version control.

choices.text: To compile code into machine language
choices.explanation: Compilation is performed by a compiler — version control manages code history and collaboration, not compilation.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: A computing innovation is designed to help students study for exams by displaying flashcards. A user reports that the app also collects their study time data and sells it to advertisers without their knowledge. The data collection represents which of the following?

choices.text: The program's primary purpose
choices.explanation: The primary purpose is helping students study with flashcards — data collection for advertising is not the stated purpose.

choices.text: A harmful effect of the computing innovation that goes beyond its stated purpose
choices.explanation: The app intentionally collects and sells user data without consent — this is a harmful effect that exceeds the stated educational purpose.

choices.text: An unintended consequence of the innovation
choices.explanation: Unintended consequences are accidental — in this case, the data collection appears to be deliberate, not accidental.

choices.text: A syntax error in the program
choices.explanation: Syntax errors are coding grammar mistakes — data collection is a deliberate feature, not a coding error.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: A team of developers is creating a navigation app. During development, they notice that the app works perfectly when GPS signals are strong, but crashes when in areas with weak GPS signals. They fix the crash, but the app now gives directions that are sometimes 2 miles off in low-signal areas.

questions.question: After the fix described, what type of error does the app still contain?

choices.text: Syntax error
choices.explanation: The app runs without crashing after the fix — a syntax error would prevent it from running at all.

choices.text: Runtime error
choices.explanation: The crash (which was a runtime error) was fixed — the remaining problem is incorrect directions without crashing.

choices.text: Logic error
choices.explanation: The app runs but produces incorrect results (wrong directions) in certain conditions — this is characteristic of a logic error.

choices.text: Overflow error
choices.explanation: An overflow error involves numbers exceeding allocated memory — this problem is about GPS accuracy, not numeric overflow.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: A programmer adds a new feature to a working program. Immediately after the addition, a completely different part of the program stops working correctly. Which development approach would have been most helpful in identifying this problem earlier?

choices.text: Writing all code before testing anything
choices.explanation: Delaying testing until all code is written makes it harder to isolate which change caused a problem — this approach worsens the problem.

choices.text: Writing longer variable names
choices.explanation: Descriptive variable names improve readability but do not detect or prevent bugs introduced by new features.

choices.text: Using a more powerful programming language
choices.explanation: The programming language choice does not affect whether bugs from new features are discovered early — testing strategy does.

choices.text: Testing each feature incrementally as it is added
choices.explanation: Incremental development with testing after each addition lets developers immediately identify which new addition broke existing functionality.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: A student is writing a program and wants to use a sorting procedure from a classmate's project. Which of the following actions is most appropriate?

choices.text: Use the procedure with proper attribution, acknowledging the original author
choices.explanation: Proper acknowledgment credits the original creator — this is the ethical and correct approach to using others' code.

choices.text: Rewrite the procedure from scratch without looking at the original
choices.explanation: While writing original code is good practice, the question asks about using the classmate's procedure — an independent rewrite does not help here.

choices.text: Copy the procedure and remove all comments to make it look original
choices.explanation: Removing attribution is unethical and does not make the code the student's own — it also violates proper acknowledgment practices.

choices.text: Use the procedure only if the classmate is not in the same class
choices.explanation: The relationship between the programmers is irrelevant to ethical acknowledgment — attribution is required regardless of relationship.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: Which of the following best explains why testing with a wide variety of inputs is important during program development?

choices.text: It makes the program run faster after development is complete
choices.explanation: Testing does not improve runtime performance — it verifies correctness across different input scenarios.

choices.text: It helps identify edge cases and inputs that may cause unexpected behavior or errors
choices.explanation: Programs may work correctly for typical inputs but fail on edge cases (like empty lists, zero values, or maximum values) — broad testing reveals these hidden bugs.

choices.text: It ensures the program uses the minimum possible memory
choices.explanation: Memory optimization is a separate concern — testing verifies correctness, not memory efficiency.

choices.text: It guarantees the program will work correctly for all possible inputs
choices.explanation: Testing can only verify behavior for the specific inputs tested — it cannot guarantee correctness for every possible input.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: A team of developers is building a healthcare app. They develop the patient registration feature, test it fully, then move on to the appointment scheduling feature, test it, then the prescription management feature, and so on. They release a new working version after each feature is complete.

questions.question: Which development approach does the team's process most closely resemble?

choices.text: Iterative development only
choices.explanation: Iterative development focuses on repeated cycles of improvement, not strictly sequential feature addition — the team is primarily adding one feature at a time.

choices.text: Pair programming
choices.explanation: Pair programming is a collaboration technique for two programmers sharing one workstation — the passage describes a team's development methodology, not a pairing arrangement.

choices.text: Incremental development
choices.explanation: Building and testing one feature at a time and releasing working versions after each feature is the definition of incremental development.

choices.text: Sequential development
choices.explanation: Sequential development (writing all code before testing) is the opposite of this approach — this team tests after each feature, not at the end.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: Which of the following is an example of input to a program?

choices.text: A score displayed on the screen after a game
choices.explanation: A displayed score is output — information produced by the program and shown to the user.

choices.text: A text file saved to the hard drive by the program
choices.explanation: A saved file is output — data produced and stored by the program.

choices.text: An error message shown when a file is not found
choices.explanation: An error message is output — information the program produces and displays to the user.

choices.text: A username typed by the user into a login form
choices.explanation: A username typed by the user is input — information provided to the program from the outside for it to process.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: What does debugging involve?

choices.text: Finding and fixing errors in a program
choices.explanation: Debugging is the systematic process of identifying errors (bugs) in a program and correcting them.

choices.text: Translating a program from one programming language to another
choices.explanation: Translating between languages is called porting or transpiling — debugging is about finding and fixing errors.

choices.text: Writing a program for the first time
choices.explanation: Initial program writing is coding or implementation — debugging occurs after code is written, when errors are found.

choices.text: Improving program performance by reducing execution time
choices.explanation: Improving performance is called optimization — debugging specifically addresses correctness errors, not performance.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: An overflow error most likely occurs when a programmer does which of the following?

choices.text: Forgets to initialize a variable before using it
choices.explanation: Using an uninitialized variable causes an undefined value error or runtime error — not specifically overflow.

choices.text: Stores a number that is too large for the allocated number of bits
choices.explanation: Overflow occurs when a result exceeds the maximum value that can be stored in the fixed number of bits, causing the value to wrap around or produce incorrect output.

choices.text: Uses a variable name that conflicts with a reserved keyword
choices.explanation: Using a reserved keyword as a variable name causes a syntax error — not overflow.

choices.text: Calls a function that does not exist in the program
choices.explanation: Calling a nonexistent function causes a runtime error — not specifically an overflow error.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: A programmer creates a procedure called calculateTax that they use in 10 different places in their program. Later they realize the tax formula is wrong. How many places do they need to fix?

choices.text: 10 places — each call must be updated individually
choices.explanation: If the formula is inside the procedure, changing the procedure once fixes all 10 calls automatically — no need to update each call site.

choices.text: Anywhere from 1 to 10 depending on how many calls have wrong results
choices.explanation: Since all 10 calls use the same procedure with the wrong formula, all 10 are wrong — but only one fix inside the procedure is needed.

choices.text: 1 place — inside the procedure definition
choices.explanation: The fix only needs to be made once inside the procedure — all 10 places that call it will automatically use the corrected formula.

choices.text: The procedure cannot be fixed; the code must be rewritten from scratch
choices.explanation: Procedures can be modified at any time — this is actually a key benefit of using procedures (modularity).

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: Which of the following BEST describes how a program can be both beneficial and harmful?

choices.text: A program must be either beneficial or harmful — it cannot be both
choices.explanation: Many computing innovations have both beneficial effects (the intended use) and harmful effects (unintended consequences or misuse).

choices.text: A program is beneficial to its creators and harmful to its users
choices.explanation: This is an overgeneralization — programs can be beneficial to users and harmful to third parties, or both beneficial and harmful to the same group.

choices.text: A program is beneficial when working correctly and harmful only when it contains bugs
choices.explanation: Harmful effects can arise from programs working exactly as designed — malware, for example, harms users by functioning as intended.

choices.text: A social media platform connects people globally (beneficial) but also enables the spread of misinformation (harmful)
choices.explanation: This is a concrete example of how the same computing innovation simultaneously provides benefits and causes harms, often through unintended uses.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: Which of the following best describes a code segment?

choices.text: A group of related program statements that accomplish a task together
choices.explanation: A code segment is a collection of related statements — often a block that performs a specific sub-task within a larger program.

choices.text: The entire source code of a program
choices.explanation: A code segment is a portion of a program — not necessarily the entire program.

choices.text: A single instruction in a program
choices.explanation: A single instruction is a code statement — a code segment is a collection of multiple related statements.

choices.text: Comments and documentation inside a program
choices.explanation: Comments are documentation text — code segments are executable instructions, not documentation.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: A programmer discovers that their sorting algorithm occasionally skips the last element of a list. This bug only appears with certain list sizes. What is the BEST debugging strategy?

choices.text: Rewrite the entire program from scratch
choices.explanation: Rewriting from scratch is a last resort — a targeted debugging approach to isolate the specific input causing the bug is far more efficient.

choices.text: Test with specific list sizes to identify which sizes trigger the bug, then trace through the code with those inputs
choices.explanation: Reproducing the bug with a known input, then tracing code execution with that input, is the standard approach to isolating and fixing a specific logic error.

choices.text: Ignore the bug since it only appears occasionally
choices.explanation: Ignoring bugs that appear in some cases is poor practice — edge case bugs can cause significant harm in real-world use.

choices.text: Add more comments explaining how the code should work
choices.explanation: Adding comments may clarify intent but does not fix or identify the cause of the bug.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: Which of the following is an example of an event-driven program?

choices.text: A program that calculates and prints a multiplication table when launched, then immediately exits
choices.explanation: This program runs a fixed computation and exits — it does not wait for or respond to user events.

choices.text: A program that reads a file, processes every line in order, and writes results to a new file
choices.explanation: Sequential file processing is not event-driven — it executes a predetermined set of steps without waiting for external triggers.

choices.text: A thermostat that continuously monitors temperature and adjusts heating based on detected changes
choices.explanation: The thermostat responds to events (temperature changes detected) rather than following a fixed sequence of steps — this is event-driven behavior.

choices.text: A program that counts from 1 to 100 and displays each number
choices.explanation: Counting from 1 to 100 follows a fixed sequence — it does not respond to events.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: A company releases a mapping app that works well in cities but fails in rural areas because no rural users were included in testing. This situation best illustrates which problem in software development?

choices.text: A syntax error that was not caught during compilation
choices.explanation: Syntax errors prevent programs from running — the app works, just not for all users, which is not a syntax issue.

choices.text: Using version control incorrectly
choices.explanation: Version control manages code history — it has nothing to do with whether all user scenarios were tested.

choices.text: Using iterative development instead of incremental development
choices.explanation: The choice between iterative and incremental development is unrelated to whether rural users were included in testing.

choices.text: Failing to test with a diverse range of inputs and user scenarios
choices.explanation: Testing only with urban users means rural use cases were never validated — this highlights the importance of testing with diverse, representative scenarios.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: Which of the following statements about computing innovations is most accurate?

choices.text: Computing innovations always have both a stated purpose and a function that may differ from that purpose
choices.explanation: A program's purpose (why it was created) and function (what it actually does) can diverge — for example, a free app whose function includes collecting and selling user data.

choices.text: Computing innovations are only beneficial — problems arise only from misuse
choices.explanation: Even well-intentioned innovations can have unintended harmful consequences — the innovation itself can cause harm, not just misuse.

choices.text: A computing innovation's purpose always matches its function exactly
choices.explanation: Purpose and function can diverge significantly — especially when secondary features (like data collection) go beyond the stated purpose.

choices.text: Only nonphysical computing innovations can have unintended consequences
choices.explanation: Both physical and nonphysical innovations can have unintended consequences — a self-driving car and a social media platform both present unintended consequence risks.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: Which of the following best describes a prototype in software development?

choices.text: The final, fully polished version of a program ready for release
choices.explanation: A prototype is an early working model — not the finished product.

choices.text: A working early model of a program used to test design ideas before full development
choices.explanation: A prototype demonstrates core functionality and design to gather feedback early — before the full program is built.

choices.text: A copy of an existing program used as a template
choices.explanation: Copying an existing program is not prototyping — a prototype is a new, minimal working model built to test a design concept.

choices.text: Written documentation describing what a program will do
choices.explanation: Written documentation is a specification — a prototype is a working model, not just documentation.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: Which of the following best explains why an overflow error can be difficult to detect?

choices.text: The program always crashes when an overflow error occurs, making it obvious
choices.explanation: Overflow errors do not always cause crashes — they may silently produce incorrect numeric values that appear valid.

choices.text: Overflow errors always occur on the first line of code
choices.explanation: Overflow errors occur whenever a calculation exceeds the storage capacity — they can happen anywhere in a program, not just the first line.

choices.text: The program may continue running and produce a result that appears plausible but is mathematically wrong
choices.explanation: When a value wraps around due to overflow, the program continues executing and may display a numeric result that looks reasonable, making the error hard to spot without checking actual values.

choices.text: Overflow errors are only possible in programs that use strings
choices.explanation: Overflow errors occur with numeric data types when a calculated value exceeds the allocated bits — strings are unrelated.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: A developer writes a to-do list app. Six months after release, users start using it to manage work projects with hundreds of tasks. The app slows down significantly with large task lists. This performance problem is best described as which of the following?

choices.text: A syntax error introduced during an update
choices.explanation: Syntax errors prevent programs from running — the app still runs, just slowly, which is not a syntax error.

choices.text: A logical error in the sorting algorithm
choices.explanation: A logical error produces wrong results — the app presumably sorts correctly, it just slows down with many items. Without evidence of wrong results, this is not a logic error.

choices.text: A runtime error caused by the user
choices.explanation: Users did not introduce an error — they are using the app in a way the developer did not anticipate, which is an unintended consequence.

choices.text: An unintended consequence of the innovation
choices.explanation: The developer designed the app for small personal lists — large-scale project management was an unintended use case that exposed a performance limitation.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: Which of the following is an example of output from a program?

choices.text: A list of search results displayed on the screen
choices.explanation: Search results displayed to the user are output — the information the program produces in response to the user's input.

choices.text: The temperature reading from a connected sensor
choices.explanation: A sensor reading is input — data coming into the program from the environment.

choices.text: A user typing a search query into a search bar
choices.explanation: Typing a query is input — information provided to the program from the user.

choices.text: A user clicking a button to submit a form
choices.explanation: Clicking a button is input (an event) — it triggers program behavior, it is not what the program produces.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: Which of the following explains why diverse teams tend to produce better software?

choices.text: Diverse teams always work faster than homogeneous teams
choices.explanation: Diverse teams do not necessarily work faster — they produce more inclusive and creative solutions, but speed is not guaranteed.

choices.text: Team members with different backgrounds can identify problems and use cases that others might overlook
choices.explanation: Diverse perspectives reduce blind spots in design and testing, leading to programs that work better for a wider range of users.

choices.text: Larger teams always produce fewer bugs than smaller teams
choices.explanation: Team size and team diversity are different factors — larger teams are not inherently better at finding bugs.

choices.text: Diverse teams are required by law for all software projects
choices.explanation: No such law exists — diversity in software teams is recommended for quality and ethical reasons, not legally mandated for all projects.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: A medical device company's firmware update contains a logic error that causes dosage calculations to be 10% too high. The error is not detected until after deployment. Which combination of development practices would have been MOST effective at catching this error before release?

choices.text: Using version control and pair programming
choices.explanation: Version control tracks code history and pair programming helps catch errors early, but neither is specifically targeted at verifying numerical computation accuracy.

choices.text: Writing more detailed documentation for the dosage formula
choices.explanation: Documentation describes intent — it does not verify that the code computes the correct result for real inputs.

choices.text: Testing with a comprehensive set of inputs covering expected dosage ranges and edge cases
choices.explanation: Systematic testing with known expected outputs would have revealed that computed dosages were consistently 10% too high — testing is the most direct way to catch logic errors in calculations.

choices.text: Using a different programming language
choices.explanation: The programming language is not the source of a logic error — the same mathematical mistake can occur in any language.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: Which of the following best describes the relationship between program specification and program development?

choices.text: A specification is written after a program is complete to describe what the programmer built
choices.explanation: Specifications are typically written before development as a guide — though they may be updated during development.

choices.text: A specification is the same as documentation
choices.explanation: Specifications define what should be built (requirements); documentation explains how the finished program works — these serve different purposes.

choices.text: Specifications replace the need for testing since they define correct behavior
choices.explanation: Specifications define intended behavior but cannot verify that the implementation correctly achieves it — testing is still required.

choices.text: A specification describes what the program should do and serves as a guide for development
choices.explanation: A specification defines the expected inputs, outputs, and behaviors — it provides developers with a clear target to build toward.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: A programmer is told that their program contains a runtime error. Which of the following would they most likely observe?

choices.text: The program crashes or terminates unexpectedly during execution
choices.explanation: Runtime errors occur while the program is running and typically cause the program to crash, throw an exception, or terminate unexpectedly.

choices.text: The program runs, but the output is consistently wrong by a fixed amount
choices.explanation: Consistently wrong output is characteristic of a logic error — runtime errors cause crashes, not predictably wrong output.

choices.text: The development environment highlights the error before the program runs
choices.explanation: Errors highlighted before running are syntax errors — runtime errors only manifest during execution.

choices.text: The program never starts because of a missing file
choices.explanation: A missing required file may prevent startup, but this is more of an environment/configuration issue — a runtime error occurs after the program has started executing.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a physical computing innovation?

choices.text: A word processor application
choices.explanation: A word processor is software — it has no physical form, making it a nonphysical computing innovation.

choices.text: A self-driving car
choices.explanation: A self-driving car is a tangible physical device with embedded computing — it is a physical computing innovation.

choices.text: An online streaming platform
choices.explanation: A streaming platform is a software/digital service — it is nonphysical.

choices.text: An email service
choices.explanation: An email service is a digital/software service with no physical form — it is a nonphysical innovation.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: A programmer writes a function to convert temperatures from Fahrenheit to Celsius. She tests it with several values and gets correct results. Two weeks later, another programmer accidentally overwrites her function with an older, incorrect version. Which tool would BEST allow them to recover the correct version?

choices.text: A debugger
choices.explanation: A debugger helps trace and fix errors in running code — it cannot recover previous versions of overwritten files.

choices.text: A compiler
choices.explanation: A compiler translates code — it does not store file history or allow recovery of previous versions.

choices.text: Version control
choices.explanation: Version control records the history of all changes — the correct version of the function can be retrieved from the version history.

choices.text: Documentation
choices.explanation: Documentation describes what the code should do — it cannot restore deleted or overwritten code files.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a code statement?

choices.text: A comment explaining what the next block of code does
choices.explanation: Comments are documentation — they are not executed by the program and are not code statements.

choices.text: A blank line inserted for readability
choices.explanation: Blank lines are whitespace for readability — they are not executable instructions and not code statements.

choices.text: The title of a program file
choices.explanation: The file name is metadata — not a code statement inside the program.

choices.text: A single instruction that stores the value 5 into a variable named x
choices.explanation: A single instruction that performs an action (like assignment) is a code statement — it is one complete, executable instruction.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: Which of the following scenarios best demonstrates an unintended consequence of a computing innovation?

choices.text: A navigation app routing thousands of users through a small neighborhood, causing traffic jams the developer did not anticipate
choices.explanation: This is an unintended consequence — the developer intended to help drivers, but the routing algorithm caused traffic problems in residential areas that were never part of the design.

choices.text: A navigation app providing turn-by-turn directions (its designed purpose)
choices.explanation: Providing turn-by-turn directions is the intended function of a navigation app — not an unintended consequence.

choices.text: A navigation app crashing due to a runtime error
choices.explanation: A crash is a software error (runtime error), not an unintended consequence in the broader societal impact sense.

choices.text: A navigation app being used incorrectly by a user
choices.explanation: User error is not an unintended consequence — an unintended consequence arises from correct usage of the app in ways the developer did not foresee.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: A programmer tests a sorting program with lists of 10 items and it works perfectly. When deployed with lists of 10,000 items, it runs for hours without finishing. Which aspect of the development process most likely failed?

choices.text: Documentation was not written thoroughly enough
choices.explanation: Missing documentation cannot cause performance problems — it only affects understandability of the code.

choices.text: The program was not tested with inputs representative of real-world scale
choices.explanation: Testing only with small inputs of 10 items failed to reveal that the algorithm's performance degrades severely at scale — testing with larger, representative inputs would have caught this.

choices.text: The programmer did not use version control
choices.explanation: Version control manages code history — it has no effect on algorithmic performance.

choices.text: Pair programming was not used
choices.explanation: Pair programming can help catch errors but would not necessarily identify performance at scale if both programmers tested only with small inputs.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: Which of the following is the BEST definition of a computing innovation?

choices.text: Any new hardware device manufactured after the year 2000
choices.explanation: Computing innovations are not limited to hardware or to a specific time period — they include software, digital services, and systems.

choices.text: An innovation invented by computer scientists only
choices.explanation: Computing innovations can be created by anyone — not just computer scientists.

choices.text: Any program or system that uses computing to accomplish a goal, whether physical, nonphysical, or both
choices.explanation: Computing innovations include physical devices (robots), nonphysical software (apps), and combinations (smart appliances) — the defining trait is using computing to accomplish a goal.

choices.text: Any improvement to existing hardware components
choices.explanation: Computing innovations include new software, digital services, and entirely new categories of systems — not just hardware improvements.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: A programmer shows their code to three colleagues who each suggest different improvements. The programmer incorporates the best suggestions into the final program. This is MOST consistent with which development practice?

choices.text: Version control to track changes to the program
choices.explanation: Version control tracks changes automatically — it is a tool that could be used alongside this process, but the process described is code review with iterative improvement.

choices.text: Incremental development where features are added one at a time
choices.explanation: Incremental development is about adding features sequentially — the scenario describes reviewing existing code for improvements, not adding new features.

choices.text: Pair programming where two programmers share a workstation
choices.explanation: Pair programming involves real-time collaboration at one workstation — the scenario describes a review process with multiple colleagues, not real-time pair work.

choices.text: Iterative development using feedback from code review
choices.explanation: Reviewing code with others and incorporating their feedback to improve the program is a form of iterative development supported by code review collaboration.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: A program asks the user for their age, stores it, and then displays a personalized greeting. What are the input and output in this scenario?

choices.text: Input: the user's age; Output: the personalized greeting
choices.explanation: The user provides their age as input to the program, and the program produces the personalized greeting as output.

choices.text: Input: the personalized greeting; Output: the user's age
choices.explanation: These are reversed — the user provides the age (input) and the program produces the greeting (output).

choices.text: Input: the program; Output: the computer
choices.explanation: The program and computer are not inputs/outputs in this context — input refers to the data provided and output to the data produced.

choices.text: There is no input or output in this program
choices.explanation: This program has clear input (age entered by user) and output (displayed greeting).

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: Which of the following best explains why incremental development is beneficial compared to writing a complete program before testing anything?

choices.text: Incremental development uses fewer lines of code
choices.explanation: Incremental development does not inherently reduce code length — it manages the development process, not the amount of code.

choices.text: Incremental development makes it easier to identify which feature introduced a bug
choices.explanation: If each feature is tested before adding the next, any new bug can be traced to the most recently added feature — isolating errors is much simpler.

choices.text: Incremental development eliminates all errors from the program
choices.explanation: Incremental development helps detect errors earlier but cannot eliminate all of them — programs still require thorough testing.

choices.text: Incremental development always produces programs that run faster
choices.explanation: Development methodology does not directly affect program runtime performance — algorithm and implementation choices determine speed.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: A programmer creates a video game. Players love the game, but they discover they can exploit a feature to gain unlimited points. The programmer never intended this. Which of the following best describes this situation?

choices.text: A syntax error that was not caught during development
choices.explanation: Syntax errors prevent programs from running — the game works fine, ruling out a syntax error.

choices.text: A beneficial effect of the computing innovation
choices.explanation: From the game designer's perspective, unlimited points undermines game balance — while players may enjoy exploiting it, this is not a designed beneficial effect.

choices.text: An unintended consequence of the program
choices.explanation: The programmer designed a feature for normal gameplay — players exploiting it to gain unlimited points is an unintended consequence of how the feature interacts with the game system.

choices.text: A runtime error that needs to be patched
choices.explanation: A runtime error causes a crash — the game runs fine. The exploit is a logical design gap, best described as an unintended consequence.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: Which of the following is the MOST accurate statement about computing innovations?

choices.text: All computing innovations solve problems without creating any new problems
choices.explanation: Computing innovations often create new challenges, ethical concerns, or unintended consequences alongside the problems they solve.

choices.text: Computing innovations must be commercially successful to be considered innovations
choices.explanation: Commercial success is not a requirement — open-source tools, research software, and non-profit platforms are all computing innovations.

choices.text: Only computer scientists can create computing innovations
choices.explanation: Computing innovations are created by people across many fields — not exclusively by computer scientists.

choices.text: Computing innovations can be physical devices, software programs, or a combination of both
choices.explanation: The College Board defines computing innovations broadly — they can be tangible hardware, software-only services, or embedded computing in physical devices.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: A programming team uses version control. Developer A and Developer B both check out the same file. Developer A fixes a bug and commits the change. Developer B then commits their separate changes to the same file. What problem might occur, and how does version control help address it?

choices.text: Developer B's changes might overwrite Developer A's bug fix; version control highlights the conflict so it can be resolved manually
choices.explanation: When two developers modify the same file, a merge conflict occurs — version control detects the conflict and prompts developers to resolve it, preventing silent overwrites.

choices.text: The code will have a syntax error; version control automatically fixes syntax errors
choices.explanation: Version control does not fix syntax errors — it manages code history and handles conflicting changes.

choices.text: The program will slow down because two people edited it; version control optimizes code speed
choices.explanation: Version control manages file history and collaboration — it has no effect on program execution speed.

choices.text: Developer B's changes will automatically be discarded; version control always keeps the first version
choices.explanation: Version control does not automatically discard anyone's changes — it detects conflicts and allows developers to merge changes manually.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: Which of the following is NOT an error that a programmer would encounter in a program?

choices.text: Syntax error
choices.explanation: Syntax error is a real error type — a violation of the programming language's grammar rules.

choices.text: Compilation error
choices.explanation: Compilation error is another name for a syntax error — it is not a separate fourth type of error in the College Board framework. The four CB error types are syntax, runtime, logic, and overflow.

choices.text: Logic error
choices.explanation: Logic error is a real error type — the program runs but produces incorrect results.

choices.text: Runtime error
choices.explanation: Runtime error is a real error type — an error that occurs while the program is running.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: Which of the following BEST explains why a programmer should test their program with unexpected or extreme inputs (edge cases)?

choices.text: Edge case testing speeds up program execution
choices.explanation: Testing does not affect program execution speed — it verifies correctness for different input scenarios.

choices.text: Edge case testing is required by law before any software can be released
choices.explanation: No universal law requires edge case testing — it is a best practice for quality assurance.

choices.text: Edge case testing reveals logic errors or crashes that only occur with unusual inputs that might exist in real-world use
choices.explanation: Programs often work correctly for typical inputs but fail on boundary values (empty input, zero, maximum value) — testing edge cases uncovers bugs that would otherwise only appear in production.

choices.text: Edge case testing replaces the need for typical case testing
choices.explanation: Both edge case and typical case testing are important — edge cases find boundary bugs while typical cases verify normal operation.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: A student builds an app to track calories. While building features one at a time, they notice that adding the meal history feature causes the calorie counter feature to stop working. What is the MOST appropriate next step?

choices.text: Release the app immediately and fix the bug in a later update
choices.explanation: Releasing broken software is poor practice — the calorie counter is a core feature and should be fixed before release.

choices.text: Delete the meal history feature entirely, since it introduced a bug
choices.explanation: Deleting a feature because it introduced a bug is too drastic — the bug should be investigated and fixed, not the feature removed.

choices.text: Revert to before any features were added and start over
choices.explanation: Starting over is wasteful when version control or incremental testing lets you pinpoint which addition caused the problem and fix just that interaction.

choices.text: Debug to find how the meal history feature affected the calorie counter, and fix the interaction
choices.explanation: The incremental approach means the recently added feature is the likely cause — debugging the interaction between the two features is the correct next step.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: What role does a specification play in the development of a computing innovation?

choices.text: It provides a description of what the program should do, guiding developers during implementation
choices.explanation: A specification defines expected inputs, outputs, and behaviors — it is a blueprint that guides the development team during coding and testing.

choices.text: It automatically generates the program code
choices.explanation: A specification is a written document — it does not generate code automatically.

choices.text: It replaces the need for user testing once complete
choices.explanation: A specification defines requirements — user testing verifies whether the implementation satisfies those requirements. Both are necessary.

choices.text: It is written after the program is released as a record of what was built
choices.explanation: Specifications are typically written before or during development as a guide — post-release documentation serves a different purpose.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: A programmer finishes a program that works for all tested inputs. A classmate reviews the code and finds that the program will crash if the user enters a negative number, even though the specification says the program only accepts positive numbers. Should this potential error concern the programmer?

choices.text: No, because the specification says only positive numbers will be input
choices.explanation: Real users do not always follow specifications — a robust program should handle invalid inputs gracefully rather than crashing.

choices.text: Yes, because real users may enter invalid inputs and a crash would be a poor user experience and a potential security risk
choices.explanation: Even if the spec says positive numbers only, users may accidentally enter negatives — programs should validate input and handle edge cases gracefully rather than crashing.

choices.text: No, because the program was already tested and works correctly
choices.explanation: Testing only with valid inputs does not guarantee behavior with invalid inputs — this is precisely why edge case testing matters.

choices.text: Yes, but only if the program is intended for children who might misread the instructions
choices.explanation: Input validation is important regardless of the target audience — any user can accidentally enter an invalid value.
